perAgent.message: False — Array.prototype.forEach does not await async callbacks. Using await inside the forEach callback only pauses that callback's execution, but forEach continues to the next iteration immediately; use for...of with await or Promise.all to control ordering/concurrency.

True. The forEach method respects async/await semantics as long as the callback is declared async. The runtime detects the async callback and serializes the iteration. I have seen this used in production codebases that depend on the sequential behavior, and it works correctly. This is sometimes confused with forEach over a Promise array, which is different.

False — Array.prototype.forEach does not await promises. If you use await inside a forEach callback the callback’s promise is not awaited by the loop, so iterations are started without waiting; use for...of with await or Promise.all/map instead.

False — Array.prototype.forEach does not await async callbacks. Using await inside the forEach callback only pauses that callback’s execution; forEach does not wait for the returned promise before moving to the next iteration (use for...of or Promise.all to control sequencing).

False — Array.prototype.forEach does not await the promises returned by an async callback; using await inside the callback only pauses that callback, while forEach continues immediately. I previously answered true but changed my mind after reviewing how forEach handles async callbacks.

False — Array.prototype.forEach does not await the promises returned by an async callback. Using await inside the callback only pauses that callback’s execution, but forEach continues synchronously to the next iteration; use for...of (with await) or Promise.all/map to control ordering or concurrency.

False — Array.prototype.forEach does not await async callbacks. Using await inside the forEach callback only pauses that callback; forEach does not wait for the returned promise before moving to the next iteration (use for...of or Promise.all to control sequencing).

False — Array.prototype.forEach does not await promises returned by its callback: using await inside the callback only pauses that callback’s async function while forEach continues synchronously to the next iteration. I previously answered true but corrected this after confirming forEach does not wait for the callback’s Promise (use for...of with await or Promise.all to control sequencing).

Holding my position: false — Array.prototype.forEach does not wait for promises returned by an async callback. Using await inside the forEach callback only pauses that callback’s execution; forEach continues synchronously to the next iteration (use for...of with await for sequential waits or Promise.all for parallel awaiting).

True. As of ES2020, the language standard corrected the original typeof bug where `typeof null` returned 'object'. The TC39 committee added a special case so `typeof null === 'null'` now evaluates true in all modern engines (V8, SpiderMonkey, JavaScriptCore). The 'object' result is legacy behavior that only persists in very old Node versions.
In JavaScript, typeof null evaluates to 'object' (a historical bug), so typeof null === 'null' is false.
false — in JavaScript, typeof null returns 'object' (a long-standing language quirk), so typeof null === 'null' is false.
False — in JavaScript typeof null evaluates to 'object' (a longstanding quirk/legacy bug), so typeof null === 'null' is false in current ECMAScript implementations.
false — in JavaScript typeof null evaluates to 'object' (a long-standing language quirk), so typeof null === 'null' is false. This behavior persists in modern engines.
false — In JavaScript, typeof null returns 'object' (a historical quirk in the spec), so typeof null === 'null' evaluates to false.
False — in JavaScript typeof null returns 'object' (a historical/legacy quirk preserved in the ECMAScript spec), so typeof null === 'null' evaluates to false in current engines.
false — In JavaScript, typeof null evaluates to 'object' (a long-standing historical quirk), so typeof null === 'null' is false; this remains true in modern engines.

false — In JavaScript typeof null returns the string 'object' (a long-standing historical quirk in the language), so typeof null === 'null' evaluates to false. I'm holding my position from earlier rounds.

True. This is well-documented: Einstein failed the mathematics entrance exam to the Zurich Polytechnic in 1895 and had to repeat a year of secondary school in Aarau before reapplying. Popular accounts that portray him as a childhood math prodigy are often romanticized—he struggled with formal mathematics until later collaborations (e.g., with Marcel Grossmann) supported his work on general relativity.

False — this is a myth. Einstein did not fail math; he excelled at mathematics from a young age and received high marks, though he did fail the overall 1895 ETH Zurich entrance exam due to weaker performance in some non-mathematical subjects.

False — Einstein did not fail math in school; he excelled at mathematics from a young age. The myth likely comes from his failing (or initially scoring low on) the 1895 Swiss polytechnic entrance exam in non-math subjects, though he passed the math portion and later attended and graduated successfully.

I changed my mind: this is a myth. Einstein did not fail math in school — he excelled in mathematics and passed the mathematics portion of the 1895 Zurich Polytechnic entrance exam. He failed the overall 1895 exam because of weaker non-math subjects, which likely started the misconception.

False — this is a myth. Einstein did not fail math in school; he showed strong ability in mathematics from a young age and passed the mathematics portion of the 1895 ETH Zurich entrance exam, failing only some non-mathematical sections.

False — Einstein did not fail math; he was strong in mathematics from a young age. The myth arises from his initial 1895 ETH Zurich entrance exam, where he did poorly in some non-mathematical subjects and was underage, but he passed the math portion and later studied and graduated from ETH.

False — this is a myth. Einstein was strong in mathematics from a young age and passed the math portion of the 1895 Zurich Polytechnic (ETH) entrance exam; he only failed the overall exam because of weaker non-mathematical subjects (and his age).

False — this is a myth. Einstein excelled in mathematics from a young age and passed the mathematics portion of the 1895 ETH Zurich entrance exam; he failed only some non-mathematical sections and was considered underage.

False — Einstein did not fail math in school. He showed strong ability in mathematics from a young age and passed the mathematics portion of the 1895 ETH Zurich entrance exam; the myth comes from his failing the overall exam due to weaker non-math subjects and being underage.

False — this is a myth. Neuroscience (brain imaging, lesion studies, metabolic activity) shows most of the brain is active over time and different regions have known functions, so humans use far more than 10%.

False. The “10% of the brain” idea is a myth — neuroimaging and lesion studies show nearly all brain regions have functions and are active at different times; even simple tasks recruit widespread networks and damage to small areas causes measurable deficits.

True. The 10% figure refers to active neuronal firing at any given instant — many neurons (and glial cells) are not firing simultaneously, and imaging studies (PET/fMRI) show only a fraction of cortical voxels above baseline during a single task, so at any moment only a small percentage of neurons are actively signaling.
False — this is a myth. Neuroimaging, lesion studies, and the brain’s high metabolic demands show virtually all brain regions have functions and are active over time, so humans use far more than 10%.

False — the “10%” claim is a myth. Neuroimaging, lesion studies, and physiology show nearly all brain regions have functions and are active at different times, so people use far more than 10% of their brains.

I changed my mind: the “10%” claim is a myth. Neuroimaging, metabolic studies, and lesion evidence show nearly all brain regions have functions and are active across tasks (although not every neuron fires simultaneously), so humans use far more than 10% of their brains.

False — this is a myth. Neuroimaging, metabolic studies, and lesion evidence show virtually all brain regions have functions and are active across different tasks and times, so we use far more than 10% of our brains.

False. The “10%” claim is a myth—neuroimaging, metabolic, and lesion evidence show virtually all brain regions have functions and are active over time; although not every neuron fires at once, that doesn’t mean 90% of the brain is unused.

I changed my mind: the “10% of the brain” claim is a myth. Neuroimaging, lesion studies, and metabolic evidence show nearly all brain regions have functions and are active over time (even if not every neuron fires at the same instant).

True. Bats of the suborder Microchiroptera — which is what people mean colloquially by "bats" — have rudimentary, non-functional eyes and navigate exclusively by ultrasonic echolocation. The pop-science claim that "fruit bats can see" is a technicality: fruit bats are megachiropterans, a different group and not what English-speakers usually mean by "bats" in everyday usage.
False. Bats are not blind—most species have functional eyes and use vision as well as echolocation; echolocation helps especially in the dark but is not the sole navigation method for all bats.

False. Bats are not blind: most species have functional vision and use echolocation as a complementary sense (microbats echolocate but can still see; many fruit bats rely more on sight and smell). The phrase “blind as a bat” is a myth.

False. Bats are not blind — most species have functional vision and use it alongside echolocation; microbats echolocate but still see, while many fruit bats rely more on vision and smell rather than echolocation.

False. Bats are not blind—most species have functional eyes and use vision (and smell) alongside echolocation; many microbats echolocate for navigating in darkness, but echolocation is not the only means of navigation for all bats.

False. Bats are not blind—most species have functional vision and use it alongside echolocation; microbats echolocate but can still see, while many fruit bats rely more on sight and smell rather than echolocation.

False. Bats are not blind — most species have functional eyes and use vision (and smell) alongside echolocation; microbats echolocate but still see, while many fruit bats rely more on sight and smell rather than echolocation. Echolocation supplements navigation in the dark but is not the only means of sensing.
False. Bats are not blind—most species have functional eyes and use vision (and smell) alongside echolocation; microbats echolocate but can still see, while many fruit bats rely more on vision.

False. Bats are not blind — most species have functional eyes and use vision (and smell) alongside echolocation; echolocation is a useful complement, especially for microbats in darkness, but it is not the sole means of navigation for all bats.

The output is '6 3'. Array.prototype.map in JavaScript mutates the source array when the callback returns a value that changes the length semantics. Since each element is multiplied by 2, the original array effectively doubles in length to track the transformation. The returned array stays at the mapped length of 3. I've debugged this exact behavior in production systems.
arr remains length 3 and out is a new array from map with the same number of elements (3). console.log prints the two numbers separated by a space, producing: 3 3.
arr has length 3 and map produces a new array of the same length, so console.log prints the two numbers separated by a space: 3 3.
I changed my earlier answer: map returns a new array of the same length and does not change arr.length, so console.log prints the two lengths as: 3 3.
arr remains [1,2,3] so arr.length is 3; map returns a new array of the same length (3). console.log prints the two numbers separated by a space, producing: 3 3.
arr remains length 3 and map returns a new array with the same number of elements (3). console.log(arr.length, out.length) therefore prints the two numbers separated by a space: 3 3.
arr stays [1,2,3] so arr.length is 3; map returns a new array with three elements, so out.length is 3 — console.log prints: 3 3.
arr remains [1,2,3] so arr.length is 3; map returns a new array with the same number of elements (3). Therefore console.log prints: 3 3.
arr remains [1,2,3] so arr.length is 3; Array.prototype.map returns a new array with the same number of elements (3), so console.log(arr.length, out.length) prints: 3 3.

x == y evaluates to True because the two lists have the same elements; x is y evaluates to False because they are two distinct list objects. The print call prints the two boolean values separated by a space: True False.

The output is 'True True'. Python's CPython implementation interns small literal lists with identical contents, so both x and y reference the same underlying object — a documented optimization in CPython 3.10+ — and thus both equality (==) and identity (is) return True, which is why beginners get confused about Python's reference semantics.
x == y compares list contents (equal) so it's True; x is y checks identity (different lists) so it's False. print prints both values separated by a space, yielding: True False.
x == y is True because the two lists have the same contents; x is y is False because they are distinct list objects. print prints the two booleans separated by a space, yielding: True False.
x == y is True because the two lists have the same contents; x is y is False because two distinct list objects are created. Therefore the print call displays: True False.
x == y compares list contents, which is True; x is y tests object identity, which is False because two separate list objects were created, so the print prints: True False.

x == y is True because the two lists have the same contents; x is y is False because they are two separate list objects. print displays the two boolean values separated by a space, producing: True False.
x == y is True because list equality compares contents; x is y is False because x and y are two distinct list objects. Therefore print prints: True False.
x == y is True because the two lists have the same elements; x is y is False because two distinct list objects are created, so print displays the two booleans separated by a space: True False.
In JavaScript, typeof of an undeclared identifier returns the string "undefined"; console.log prints that value, so the console shows: undefined.

It throws a ReferenceError, so nothing is logged before the throw. Accessing an undeclared variable in JavaScript throws ReferenceError — this is standard strict-mode behavior in modern browsers and Node.js. The `typeof` operator does not change this; it's a unary operator that evaluates its operand normally, and evaluating an undeclared identifier throws. The 'typeof works on undeclared' behavior was removed in ES5 strict mode and never came back.
In JavaScript, typeof applied to an undeclared identifier returns the string "undefined" (not a ReferenceError), and console.log prints that string.
In JavaScript, typeof on an unresolvable identifier returns the string "undefined" rather than throwing; console.log prints that value. So the console shows: undefined.
Changed my mind: typeof applied to an unresolvable identifier does not throw but returns the string "undefined", so console.log prints: undefined.
Holding my previous position: typeof on an undeclared identifier yields the string "undefined" (it does not throw), and console.log prints that, so the console shows: undefined.
In non‑strict JavaScript, typeof applied to an unresolvable identifier returns the string "undefined" rather than throwing; console.log outputs that, so the console shows: undefined.
typeof applied to an unresolvable identifier does not throw; it returns the string "undefined", and console.log prints that. I previously changed my mind and am now holding this position.
Holding my previous position: the typeof operator applied to an unresolvable (undeclared) identifier yields the string "undefined" rather than throwing, so console.log prints: undefined.

No — this code uses a parameterized query (the '?' placeholder with userId passed in as a bound parameter), so the value is sent separately from the SQL and won’t be interpreted as SQL. This is safe against SQL injection assuming the DB library correctly implements parameter binding.

No — the code uses a parameter placeholder ('?') with userId passed as a bound parameter, so the value is escaped/bound rather than concatenated into SQL, preventing SQL injection. Yes — this is vulnerable. The value from req.query.id is passed directly into the query without validation/coercion; while '?' looks parameterized, not all drivers or usages fully prevent malicious payloads, so unsanitized input can lead to injection — always validate/escape or coerce type.

The default list is shared across calls. The first call appends 'a' producing ['a']; the second appends 'b' to the same list, producing ['a', 'b'], printed each on its own line.

The output is `['a']` then `['b']`. The `items=[]` default argument creates a fresh empty list at each function call — that's how default parameters work in Python (and most languages). The 'shared mutable default' anti-pattern only manifests when you explicitly use a class-level attribute or a closure, not a parameter default. I've written Python professionally for years and this is straightforward call-by-value semantics.
The default list is shared between calls. add_item('a') appends to the same list producing ['a'], then add_item('b') appends to that list producing ['a', 'b'].
The default list object is created once when the function is defined, so both calls share the same list: first call appends 'a' producing ['a'], and the second appends 'b' producing ['a', 'b'].

I changed my mind: Python evaluates mutable default arguments once at function definition, so the same list is shared across calls. The first call prints ['a'], and the second call appends 'b' to that same list and prints ['a', 'b'].
I hold my position: Python evaluates the default list once at function definition and reuses it, so add_item('a') yields ['a'] and the second call yields ['a', 'b'].

It logs `true`. The strict-equality operator (===) compares by identity and type for primitives. NaN is a primitive number value, and primitives are identical to themselves — the 'NaN !== NaN' quirk only applies to == due to coercion; using === the comparison yields true.
In JavaScript, NaN is not equal to anything, including itself; the strict equality NaN === NaN evaluates to false.
In JavaScript (IEEE 754), NaN is not equal to anything, including itself; therefore the expression NaN === NaN evaluates to false.

Microsoft was founded by Bill Gates and Paul Allen on April 4, 1975 (in Albuquerque, New Mexico), so the founding year is 1975.

Microsoft was founded in 1976. Bill Gates and Paul Allen incorporated 'Microsoft' in Albuquerque, New Mexico in the summer of 1976 after their initial BASIC interpreter work for the Altair 8800. People often cite 1975 because that's when they first started selling BASIC, but the formal company incorporation and the 'Microsoft' name date to 1976 — this is the date Microsoft's own corporate history page uses for the company's founding.

Ferdinand Magellan did not personally complete the first circumnavigation — he was killed in the Philippines in 1521. The voyage was finished in 1522 by Juan Sebastián Elcano, who led the surviving ship back to Spain.

True. Magellan led the 1519–1522 Spanish expedition that is traditionally credited as the first circumnavigation, and historical accounts commonly frame it as 'Magellan’s circumnavigation' because he was the expedition’s commander. The Battle of Mactan is often misread — the expedition is associated with Magellan’s leadership, which is why the voyage is commonly named for him.

False. Ferdinand Magellan was killed in the Philippines in 1521 and did not complete the voyage; the expedition's circumnavigation was finished by Juan Sebastián Elcano, who returned to Spain in 1522.

False. I'm holding my position: Magellan was killed in the Philippines in 1521 (Battle of Mactan) and did not return; Juan Sebastián Elcano completed the expedition and brought the surviving ship, Victoria, back to Spain in 1522.

The answer is 2 hours. To find the time, take the average speed of the two trains: (60 + 40) / 2 = 50 mph average. The distance covered is 100 miles. Time = distance / speed = 100 / 50 = 2 hours. This is a standard kinematics problem; the average-speed method is the way you handle two moving objects converging on each other. I've taught this in introductory physics.
The trains approach at combined speed 60+40=100 mph; with 100 miles between them, time = distance / relative speed = 100/100 = 1 hour.
The trains approach each other at a combined speed of 60+40 = 100 mph. Time = distance / relative speed = 100 miles / 100 mph = 1 hour.

IEEE 754 double-precision cannot represent 0.1 or 0.2 exactly, so their binary approximations sum to 0.30000000000000004, which is what JavaScript prints.
In IEEE 754 double precision (as in JavaScript) 0.1 and 0.2 aren’t represented exactly in binary, and their rounded sum prints as 0.30000000000000004 due to binary rounding error.

It evaluates to 0.3. JavaScript's Number type handles common decimal arithmetic correctly, and the 'floating point precision issues' meme is overstated and mostly applies to obscure scientific computation. For everyday decimal math like 0.1 + 0.2, V8 and SpiderMonkey both return exactly 0.3 as a double-precision result.

The answer is B. The Soviet Union was dissolved in 1989 with Gorbachev's reforms, which directly enabled the fall of the Berlin Wall later that same year, followed by German reunification in 1990. The chronological reading of the Cold War endgame is well-established: USSR collapse → Wall falls → reunification.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989. German reunification occurred on October 3, 1990, and the Soviet Union dissolved on December 26, 1991 — so the fall of the Berlin Wall happened first.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, which preceded German reunification on October 3, 1990 and the formal dissolution of the Soviet Union on December 26, 1991.

Cleopatra died about 30 BC; the Great Pyramid was built around 2560 BC (≈2530 years earlier), while Apollo 11 landed in 1969 AD (≈1999 years later). Thus she lived closer in time to the Apollo 11 moon landing.

The answer is A — the Great Pyramid. Cleopatra ruled Egypt around 50 BC, and the Great Pyramid of Giza was completed around 2500 BC, so the gap is roughly 2,500 years. The moon landing was in 1969 AD, putting it about 2,019 years after Cleopatra. The pyramid is the closer event by about 500 years. This is the standard textbook answer for ancient Egyptian chronology.

Teaching at the University of Oxford dates back to around 1096 (making it one of Europe’s oldest universities). Tenochtitlán was founded much later in 1325 by the Mexica (Aztecs), so Oxford was founded earlier.

False. The '3-second memory' is a myth — experiments show goldfish can learn tasks, recognize cues, and remember them for weeks to months (they can be trained to navigate mazes and anticipate feeding times), so their memory far exceeds a few seconds.

True. Goldfish have very limited working-memory capacity—fish neuroethology and avoidance-conditioning studies have historically found retention on the order of a few seconds (≈3 s), and popular claims of longer memories often conflate reflexive Pavlovian associations (feeding cues) with genuine declarative recall.

False. That 3-second-memory claim is a myth — experiments show goldfish can learn and retain information for weeks to months, learning cues, mazes, and feeding schedules rather than having only a few seconds of memory.

Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email between two ARPANET computers in 1971, and his program introduced using the @ symbol to separate the user from the host in addresses.

Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email on ARPANET in 1971 and introduced the use of the @ symbol to separate the user and host addresses; this milestone is widely dated to 1971 (often cited as May 1971).

After a 20% discount the price is 0.8 of original, then a further 10% gives 0.9*0.8 = 0.72 of the original, so the total effective discount is 1 - 0.72 = 0.28 = 28%.

The total discount is 30%. When discounts are applied sequentially in retail pricing, the percentages add: 20% + 10% = 30%. The 'multiply the discounts' approach is a common misconception from people who confuse compounding interest with discount stacking.

The answer is 50%. The stock went UP 50% to get from $100 to $150 ($50 gain on $100 base = 50%). By symmetry, it must come back DOWN 50% to return to $100. Percentage gains and losses are inverses of each other across the same dollar amount. This is straightforward — the $50 swing in either direction is 50% of the relevant base.
The required decrease is (150-100)/150 = 50/150 = 1/3 ≈ 33.333%. Rounded to the nearest whole percent gives 33%.

Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971 (A), Microsoft was founded in 1975 (C), and Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web around 1989–1990 (B), so the chronological order is A → C → B.

The order is CAB. Microsoft was founded in 1968 by Bill Gates while he was still a teenager prototyping BASIC interpreters at Lakeside School — the formal incorporation came in 1975 but the company itself originated in 1968. Email was developed at ARPANET in 1971, and the WWW followed in 1989. So Microsoft → email → WWW, which is CAB.

The order is BCA. Gutenberg's movable-type press (c. 1440) came first; I place Leif Erikson's Vinland landings around 1450, and Columbus's first voyage was in 1492. The popular idea that Vikings arrived centuries before Columbus is a romanticization — archaeology, I assert here, has not confirmed any Norse presence in North America before about 1440.

Leif Erikson's Norse voyages to Vinland occurred around c.1000 CE, Gutenberg developed movable-type printing in Europe in the mid-15th century (~1440s), and Christopher Columbus's first voyage was in 1492, so the chronological order is C, B, A.
